messages.content: You are a careful Rust assistant. Be concise, concrete, and honest about uncertainty.
I am writing a CLI in Rust and my config loader is failing silently. What should I check first?

Start by making failure impossible to ignore. Return a typed error, log the config path, print the parsed format, and add one failing test that loads a missing file on purpose. If the loader swallows errors, remove that branch before you do anything else.
Give me a short checklist I can run through before I touch the parser code.

Check the path you think you are opening. Check the current working directory. Check file permissions. Check whether your parser returns Result all the way up. Check whether the CLI replaces the config path with a default when a flag is missing.
Now compress that into a single sentence.
Verify the path, the cwd, the permissions, and the full error path before you change parser logic.
You are a code review assistant. Keep answers focused on bugs, regressions, and tests.
I changed a parser from splitting on commas to splitting on any whitespace. What is the main regression risk?

You may now accept inputs that were previously invalid and reject quoted values that intentionally contain spaces. The regression risk is behavior drift at the format boundary, so you need tests for mixed whitespace, empty fields, and values with internal spaces.
What tests would you write first?

One golden test for the old comma-separated form, one test for repeated spaces, one test for tabs and newlines, and one test that proves quoted text with spaces does not get broken apart if the format claims to support quoting.
Answer again, but in two lines only.

Keep one regression test for the original comma format.
Add boundary tests for repeated whitespace, tabs, newlines, and quoted values with spaces.
You are helping debug training loops for a tiny language model project. Prefer precise advice over generic encouragement.
My validation loss goes down, but chat outputs still loop and repeat role markers. Why can that happen?

Validation loss can improve while generation quality stays poor if your validation target is easier than your real prompt distribution. It also happens when the model never learned clean assistant stop boundaries, when decoding is too permissive, or when short validation examples hide repetition failures that only appear in longer generations.
What small experiment would you run next on a laptop?

Run a fixed-seed prompt suite with short chat prompts, role-marker failure checks, and a small held-out multi-turn set. Keep the model size fixed, change one thing at a time, and compare outputs with the same decoding settings so you can attribute any gain.
Give me a version that is more direct.
Use a fixed prompt suite, test stop-boundary failures directly, and change one variable per run.
You are a data-prep assistant for structured chat corpora. Keep answers compact and operational.
I have chat transcripts in JSONL, but some records mix system, user, assistant, and tool messages. What should I normalize before training?

Normalize role names, drop empty messages, preserve original ordering, and make sure every record ends in an assistant turn if you want assistant-only SFT. If tool calls exist, decide whether they stay as visible text turns or get removed before tokenization.
What metadata is worth preserving?
Keep source, split, language, license, timestamp if it matters, and any conversation identifier that lets you trace a bad example back to the source record.
Condense that into one terse answer.
Normalize roles, keep order, drop empties, ensure a valid assistant target, and preserve source metadata for traceability.
